details.text: Derivative: رَبّ (Rabb) linguistically contains several implications. The primary implied meaning is “the absolute owner” like the owner of a slave or a house. رَبّ (Rabb) also implies the سَيِّد (Sayyid) who is the one who has complete authority and is the master of something and completely in charge of it. Because of the closeness with the verb رَبَّى it has been said that رَبّ (Rabb) also implies the meaning of the مُرَبِّي (Murabbiy) who is the one who looks after and cares for something to make sure that it develops correctly and soundly until it reaches its desired result. رَبّ (Rabb) has also been said to imply the meaning of the مُرْشِد (Al-Murshid) which refers to the one who guides and sets something in the right direction and the meaning of the مُنْعِم (Al-Mun’im) the one who grants favours and gifts and finally the قَيِّم (Al-Qayyim) which implies the one who ensures the existence of the subject i.e., our existence as subjects is only because Allah allows it.

Derivative: النَّاسِ – The word النَّاسِ (An-Naas- People) and the word إِنْسان (person or human) has been said to come from the root نَسِيَ – نِسْيَان which means to forget. As such when it is used, the forgetful nature of the human being is implied within its meaning.

Derivative: مَلِكِ (Malik) refers to the authority of Allah i.e., the one who has complete control over the affairs of people to dispose with as He will. It is used in Arabic to refer to a king who has authority and dominion.

Derivative: إِلَٰهِ is derived from the word أَلِهَ (Aliha) which can imply to stick, cling and resort (or incline) to someone or something. This is part of the meaning of إِلَٰهِ (Ilaah) in that He is the one that we should be constantly turning to and seeking to be connected to. إِلَٰهِ (Ilaah) implies the meaning of the One who should be worshipped, the One who should be obeyed and the One to whom subservience is due.

Derivative: الْوَسْوَاسِ is from the verb وَسْوَسَ which means to whisper the use of a low voice. However, unlike other words to whisper in Arabic وَسْوَسَ (Waswasa) is only used in a negative context. Also, in Arabic when a word has a repetition of letters in it like وَس (Was) and وَس (Was) here, then it alludes to an act that is repeated i.e., وَسْوَسَ refers to the act of whispering over and over again and implies repetition and continuity. The form of الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas) is not the Masdar of the verb وَسْوَسَ. The Masdar is وَسْوَسَة (Waswasah) which means whispering. الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas) here is an emphasised and hyperbolised form for the one who engages in whispering, with the meaning of someone who whispers a lot or incessantly. The use of Alif and Laam (ال) in الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas) indicates that it is a specific whisperer being referred to i.e., Shaytan. الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas) is therefore a name and description of Shaytan. As such seeking refuge and protection from the evil of الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas) implies all of his evil whether that is whispering or other than whispering.

Derivative: الْخَنَّاسِ (the one who keeps retreating) is an adjective for الْوَسْوَاسِ (Al-Waswaas – the incessant whisperer) and it is the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – emphasised and hyperbolised) form of الخُنُوس (Al-Khunoos) from the verb خَنَسَ which means to retreat. The emphasised form of الْخَنَّاسِ (Al-Khannass) implies that he keeps retreating which in turn implies that he also keeps stepping forward.

مِن شَرِّ الْوَسْوَاسِ الْخَنَّاسِ

- The first three verses of this Surah relate to “whom” the refuge and protection should be sought from whilst the last three verses beginning from this one relate to “what” the refuge and protection should be sought from.

Derivative: صُدُورِ is the plural of صَدْر (Sadr) which means “chest”. The difference between chest and heart (قَلْب) is that the heart is deeper in the chest. As such the use of the word صَدْر (Sadr- chest) here in this context implies that the whispering of Shaytan does not penetrate the heart directly or have direct access to it.

مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ

- “From the Jinn and mankind (or the people)” – It has been said that Jinn has been placed first before the mention of the people as the Jinn have precedence in respect to whispering to people and they are the origin of the whispering in the case where when people whisper to other people it is as a result of the Jinn first whispering to them. The people who whisper are as such only vehicles for the whispering of the Jinn.
